coredata.dc:title: Tuning the functional groups of carbon quantum dots in thin film nanocomposite membranes for nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Huazhen
Wu, Peiyi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials containing a carbon core with a tiny size of only several nanometers and a shell linked with functional groups, have shown great potential in fabricating high-performance water treatment membranes due to their excellent hydrophilicity, tunable size and surface properties, and favorable polymer affinity. Herein, three functionalized CQDs with carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups, respectively, were synthesized and further incorporated into the polyamide layer of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes via interfacial polymerization. The influences of functional groups of CQDs on the membrane properties were systematically investigated. Benefiting from the advantages of excellent hydrophilicity and ultra-small size of CQDs, all the TFN membranes incorporated with CQDs exhibited a higher permeate flux along with well-maintained Na2SO4 rejection efficiency and a better antifouling capacity in comparison with the thin film composite (TFC) membrane. Nonetheless, the different functional groups on CQDs also endowed these TFN membranes with different properties. Among them, the TFN-SCQD membrane possessed the highest permeate flux of 42.1 L m−2 h−1 and the Na2SO4 rejection of 93.6%. Moreover, the introduction of SCQD also endowed the TFN membrane with the best antifouling performance. Differently, the TFN-NCQD membrane exhibited a better retention of divalent cations and showed only slightly improved antifouling performance. Our investigation results reveal that the membrane properties of TFN-CQD membranes, especially separation performance, can be effectively modulated through tuning the functional groups of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Thin-film nanocomposite
Carbon quantum dot
Interfacial polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with different functional groups
Preparation of the TFC and TFN membranes
Permeation and separation properties of membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science & Technology of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of Science & Technology of China (No. 2016YFA0203302 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process of the different functionalized CQDs.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of different TFN-CQD membranes via interfacial polymerization.
TEM images of CCQD (a), NCQD (b), and SCQD (c); XPS spectra (d), FTIR spectra (e), and zeta potential (f) of different CQDs.
FTIR spectra of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes.
XPS spectra of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes.
Surface FESEM images of the TFC (a), TFN-CCQD (b), TFN-NCQD (c) and TFN-SCQD (d) membranes.
AFM three-dimensional surface images (scan size 5μm × 5μm) of the TFC (a), TFN-CCQD (b), TFN-NCQD (c) and TFN-SCQD (d) membranes.
Contact angle and zeta potential of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes.
Pure water flux (a) and salt rejection ratios at 0.6 MPa (b) of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes.
Antifouling behaviors of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes in the NF process. The time-dependent flux of BSA (a) and fouling resistance of BSA (b).
The chemical composition of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes obtained from XPS spectra.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: C/N: molar ratio of carbon to nitrogen in the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM surface roughness parameters of the TFC and TFN membranes: R a (Average Roughness) and R rms (Root Mean Square Roughness).

head.ce:title: Tuning the functional groups of carbon quantum dots in thin film nanocomposite membranes for nanofiltration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Department of Macromolecular Science and Laboratory of Advanced Materials, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Department of Macromolecular Science and Laboratory of Advanced Materials, Fudan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials containing a carbon core with a tiny size of only several nanometers and a shell linked with functional groups, have shown great potential in fabricating high-performance water treatment membranes due to their excellent hydrophilicity, tunable size and surface properties, and favorable polymer affinity. Herein, three functionalized CQDs with carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups, respectively, were synthesized and further incorporated into the polyamide layer of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes via interfacial polymerization. The influences of functional groups of CQDs on the membrane properties were systematically investigated. Benefiting from the advantages of excellent hydrophilicity and ultra-small size of CQDs, all the TFN membranes incorporated with CQDs exhibited a higher permeate flux along with well-maintained Na2SO4 rejection efficiency and a better antifouling capacity in comparison with the thin film composite (TFC) membrane. Nonetheless, the different functional groups on CQDs also endowed these TFN membranes with different properties. Among them, the TFN-SCQD membrane possessed the highest permeate flux of 42.1 L m−2 h−1 and the Na2SO4 rejection of 93.6%. Moreover, the introduction of SCQD also endowed the TFN membrane with the best antifouling performance. Differently, the TFN-NCQD membrane exhibited a better retention of divalent cations and showed only slightly improved antifouling performance. Our investigation results reveal that the membrane properties of TFN-CQD membranes, especially separation performance, can be effectively modulated through tuning the functional groups of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofiltration (NF), as a kind of membrane separation technology, is widely considered as a crucial strategy for water purification due to its advantages in simplicity, less energy consumption, and environment-friendly property [1–3]. In particular, the thin-film composite (TFC) polyamide membranes, usually fabricated by interfacial polymerization (IP) on a porous support, are being intensively explored and industrially applied [4,5]. However, the limitations of flux-selectivity trade-off and high fouling propensity still restrict their application in NF process [6–8]. Thus, the development of advanced membrane technologies and the search for novel materials are much needed.

The incorporation of nanomaterials in a dense polyamide layer to form so-called thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes is taken as an efficacious and promising solution to the current bottlenecks of conventional TFC membranes [9,10]. Through rationally selecting or designing nanomaterials, the structure and chemical properties of the membranes can be tuned to meet the need of specific water treatment applications. To date, a variety of nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [11,12], graphene oxides (GOs) [13,14], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [15,16], and graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) [17], have been introduced into a polyamide matrix via the interfacial polymerization technology. Benefitting from the distinct properties of these nanomaterials, certain TFN membranes with improved water permeability, antifouling performance, bactericidal effects or mono-/di-valent ion separation capacity have been successfully developed. Nevertheless, the nonselective interface voids that arise from the poor compatibility between the polyamide matrix and inorganic nanomaterials would frequently lead to the risk of declined solute selectivity and performance stability [18,19]. Besides, the size of nanofillers, a generally neglected factor, is also essential to the construction of organic-inorganic hybrid selective layers [20]. In fact, the sizes of nanofillers used in the previous literature are usually at least dozens of nanometers, which are much thicker than the actual barrier layer of the TFC membranes. Therefore, it is hard to ensure the real incorporation of these inorganic nanofillers or organic nanoparticles inside the barrier layer, which would significantly restrict their latent functionalities to improve membrane performance. Thus, it is quite necessary to meticulously control the size of nanofillers when attempting to fabricate high-performance TFN membranes. For examples, Singh et al. [21] tactfully chose SiO2 nanoparticles with sizes of 3 nm and 16 nm for fabricating TFN membranes in the earlier years. Most recently, Xu et al. [22,23] also wisely synthesized SiO2 nanoparticles with the diameter of about 10 nm and Au nanoparticles with the diameter of about 5 nm, and then they are introduced into the selective layer of TFN membranes, respectively. The super small size of these nanofillers made them be really embedded in the selective layer, thereby significantly improving the nanofiltration performance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials containing a carbon core with a tiny size of only several nanometers and a shell linked with carbonyl, carboxyl and other functional groups, have attracted extensive attention and also shown bright promise in the biologically related field, light-emitting diodes, fuel cells and photovoltaic devices [24–29]. Most recently, CQDs are considered as promising nanofillers for fabricating TFN membranes due to their excellent hydrophilicity, water dispersibility and ultra-small size. For examples, Wang et al. [30] firstly utilized graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD), a typical kind of CQDs which usually possess better crystallinity than its cousins [31], as the nanofiller to fabricate novel TFN reverse osmosis membranes for antifouling and chlorine resistance. Bai et al. [32] fabricated novel TFN membranes comprising GOQD through interfacial polymerization for low-pressure nanofiltration, and the resultant membranes exhibited high flux and antifouling properties. Zhang et al. [33] also systematically studied the influence of carbon dots (CDs) on polyamide TFN reverse osmosis membranes. These exciting developments show that the desired purpose of improving the multi-functional properties of polyamide TFN membranes could be readily realized. However, research studies concerning the influence of the functional groups of CQDs on the properties of the polyamide TFN membranes have rarely been reported.

Given this, we proposed to investigate how the functional groups on CQDs alter the property of the polyamide layer in TFN membranes. Herein, CQDs with three different functional groups (carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups) were meticulously synthesized, respectively. Then, a series of novel TFN nanofiltration membranes were prepared by interfacial polymerization with the incorporation of different kinds of CQDs in aqueous solutions. The fabricated polyamide TFN membranes were comprehensively analyzed by measuring the surface composition, morphology, roughness, hydrophilicity, and zeta potential. Furthermore, the membrane performance in terms of pure water flux, salt rejection, and antifouling capacity was also systematically investigated to evaluate the significant influence of the functional groups on CQDs.

Commercially available polysulfone ultrafiltration flat sheet membranes were purchased from the Development Center of Water Treatment Technology (Hangzhou, China). Poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) (PSS, M.W. 70,000) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid (CA), branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI, M. W. 1 800), trimesoyl chloride (TMC), piperazine (PIP), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and all other chemicals used were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs with different functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: Three different functionalized CQDs are carboxylic CQD (CCQD), amino CQD (NCQD) and sulfonated CQD (SCQD). The scheme of the synthesis process of these CQDs is shown in Fig. 1.

CCQD used in this study was prepared by directly pyrolyzing CA [34]. Briefly, 2 g CA was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C in a quartz tube furnace under N2 atmosphere for 2 h. During the heating process, CA was first liquated and then the color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange, implying the formation of CCQD. To purify the as-obtained product, the CCQD sample was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 1000 Da cut-off molecular weight against deionized water for 3 d. Finally, the products were lyophilized and stored in a dryer before use.

NCQD was synthesized by the one-step and low-temperature pyrolysis of CA in the presence of BPEI described in the previously reported literature [35]. To be specific, 2 g CA and 1 g BPEI was first dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. Then the solution was heated inside a 50 mL autoclave at 200 °C for 3 h. The purification and final treatment of NCQD were performed with the same method as mentioned above.

SCQD was also prepared in a one-step pyrolysis method using CA and PSS as precursors according to the previous literature [36]. In a typical procedure, 2 g CA and 0.5 g PSS were dissolved in deionized water followed by evaporation at 100 °C until a dry powder was obtained. The mixed powder was then pyrolyzed for 2 h at 200 °C in a tubular furnace under a nitrogen atmosphere. The purification and final treatment of SCQD were performed with the same method as mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of TFN membranes incorporated with different CQDs via interfacial polymerization is presented in Fig. 2 [37,38]. First, three different kinds of CQDs were dispersed in PIP aqueous solution (1.0% w/v) by ultrasonic dispersion, respectively. Here,150 mg/L is considered to be the optimum loading concentration of CQDs for fabricating TFN membranes in this work according to the results of flux and salt rejection of the TFN membranes containing CCQD contents from 0 to 300 mg/L (as shown in Fig. S1). Then, the support membranes were immersed in the aqueous solution for 5 min. The excess solution was drained from the membrane surface. Subsequently, the PIP-saturated support membranes were immersed into the organic phase which composed of 0.15% w/v TMC in cyclohexane for 1 min. After removing the residual cyclohexane, the resultant membranes were heated in 70 °C for 30 min for further polymerization and then stored in deionized water before tests. According to the kinds of CQDs, the corresponding membranes were denoted as TFN-CCQD, TFN-NCQD and TFN-SCQD, respectively. All these TFN membranes incorporated with CQDs could be denoted as TFN-CQD. For comparison purpose, the TFC membranes were also prepared under the same conditions without adding any CQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on an RBD upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Mg Ka radiation. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was measured on JEM-2100F (JEOL) with an acceleration of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was taken on Nicolet Nexus 470 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 64 scans. The zeta potential of the CQDs was measured by a nano-zeta potential analyzer (ZS90–2027). Photographs of the as-prepared CQDs solution were obtained under a UV beam of 365 nm.

Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and XPS mentioned above were used to analyze the surface chemical composition of the membranes. Both atomic force microscopy (AFM, Multimode Nano 4, tapping mode) and field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Zeiss, Ultra 55) were performed to investigate the membrane surface morphology. OCA15 (Datapyysics CO) was used to measure the static water contact angles of the membranes. Zeta potentials of the membranes were performed with the use of 0.01 M KCl solution (pH = 6.0 ± 0.2) as reported in our previous work [39].

The pure water flux and salt rejection of the TFC and TFN membranes prepared in this work were evaluated via a lab-made cross-flow filtration apparatus [40,41]. Before the tests, the membrane coupons (26.2 cm2) were pressurized at 0.8 MPa for about 20 min to make the NF system stable. Afterward, pure water fluxes were measured under different pressures (0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 MPa). The pure water flux (F) could be calculated via Eq. (1):
(1)
F =
V
A × t
where V is the volume of the permeated pure water; A is the effective membrane area, and t is the operation time.

After the pure water permeability tests, feed solutions containing 5 mmol/L concentration of either Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 or NaCl was selected as retention probes to evaluate the salt retention performance. The operation pressure was fixed at 0.6 MPa. The salt rejection (R) can be calculated using the following equation:
(2)
R = 1 −
C p
C f
where C p and C f were the concentrations of the permeation and feed solutions, respectively. The concentrations of the permeation and feed salt solutions were determined by electrical conductivity (HANA-EC215, Italy) [42]. Meanwhile, the molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of the membranes was detected by using a series of polyethylene glycol (PEG) with different molecular weights (MW = 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 Da) [23]. The operation condition was the same as described above for filtration testing.

Antifouling experiments were conducted using BSA as the model foulant according to our previous work [43]. The operation pressure here was 0.6 MPa during the whole process. Prior to the fouling experiments, 5 mmol/L Na2SO4 feed solution was first passed through the membranes for 3 h to determine the initial value of the water flux (J 1). Then, Na2SO4 (5 mmol/L) and BSA (50 mg/L) mixture as feed solution was applied to perform the fouling test for 20 h, and the steady flux for this mixed solution (J p) was recorded. Subsequently, deionized water was fed to clean the fouled membrane for 3 h at 0.6 MPa. The water flux of cleaned membranes (J 2) was measured again using Na2SO4 solution (5 mmol/L). The flux recovery ratio (FRR), and total fouling ratio (R t) were calculated via the equations (3) and (4):
(3)
F R R =
J 2
J 1
(4)
R t
=
J 1
−
J p
J 1

The morphology of the synthesized CQDs was analyzed by TEM. The images are shown in Fig. 3a-c demonstrate that all CQDs are well-dispersed and present near spherical dots-like morphology. According to Fig. S2, the average diameter of CCQD is ca. 2.7 nm with a narrow distribution. The size of NCQD and SCQD is 3.3 and 3.4 nm, respectively, which is larger than that of CCQD. Meanwhile, their size distribution becomes wider in comparison with CCQD. These phenomena might be attributed to the regulating effect of PSS or BPEI during the pyrolysis process [35].

XPS and FTIR analysis were employed to reveal the chemical nature of the functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The XPS results (Fig. 3d) show that all CQDs include the peaks of C1s (286 eV) and O1s (532 eV), which is mainly originated from the pyrolysis of CA [44]. When BPEI or PSS were introduced during the pyrolysis of CA, the new characteristic peaks at 400 eV (N1s) and 171 eV (S2p3) show up in their corresponding XPS spectra, respectively. These results imply that the functional groups of PSS or BPEI are successfully anchored onto CQDs. And the specific chemical composition of CQDs calculated from XPS spectra is listed in Table S1. The FTIR spectra further reveal the presence of specific functional groups on CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3e, all the CQDs exhibit the typical absorption peaks of the functional groups at 3410 cm−1 and around 1729 cm−1 (indicated by the dotted oval), corresponded to phenolic C–OH stretching, carbonyl/carboxylic C˭O stretching vibrations, respectively. For NCQD, the new peaks at 1554 and 1150 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and C-N of BPEI appear in its FTIR spectrum [45]. In respect to SCQD, the new peaks at 1223 and 1179 cm−1 attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of S˭O, appear in its spectrum, as well as the peak at 673 cm−1 attributed to the S-O stretching vibration [46]. Collectively, both FTIR and XPS results clearly suggest the presence of –COOH and –OH groups for all CQDs, and the presence of –NH2/-NH- groups and -SO3H groups for NCQD and SCQD, respectively. Zeta potential was measured to investigate the surface charges of these as-prepared CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3f, CCQD shows negative charge due to the presence of abundant carboxylic groups on its surface. More negative zeta potential value is observed for SCQD due to the further introduced multivalent –SO3 2- groups. Whereas, the introduction of BPEI endows the NCQD with positive charges. What's more, the aqueous solutions of all CQDs emit blue light when excited with 364 nm UV beam (Fig. S3), which complementarily confirms the successful preparation of CQDs.

The FTIR spectra of PSf support, TFC, and TFN-CQD membranes are shown in Fig. 4. Compared to the spectrum of PSf supporting membrane, the new peaks at 3416, 1620 and 1364 cm−1 correspond to O-H, C˭O and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively, show up in the FTIR spectra of both TFC and TFN-CQD membranes, strongly confirming the successful formation of a polyamide layer on the PSf supporting membranes via interfacial polymerization [47,48]. However, the characteristic peaks of CQDs are not well pronounced in the FTIR spectra of TFN-CQD membranes due to their minute amount in the PA layer and may be masked by the peaks of PSf and polyamide. Therefore, XPS was used to further investigate the surface chemical composition of the membranes and confirm the successful incorporation of CQDs.

The XPS spectra of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes are shown in Fig. 5 and the atomic concentrations of different elements of these membranes calculated from the corresponding XPS spectra are listed in Table 1. It is obvious from Fig. 5 that both TFC and TFN-CQD membranes present characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s which mainly originate from the established polyamide layer. From the quantitative analysis of XPS (Table 1), these three TFN membranes incorporated with different CQDs, especially CCQD and SCQD, possess significantly higher contents of oxygen in comparison with the TFC membrane. This should be attributed to the successful introduction of CQDs which contains abundant oxygen-containing groups. What's more, the C/N molar ratio which could be taken as an indicator for measuring the cross-linking degree of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes shows some interesting results [49–51]. Compared to the TFC membrane, the C/N ratio in TFN-CCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes, especially TFN-SCQD membrane, is obviously increased, indicating that the membrane chemical cross-linking degree is decreased. This change of the chemical structure of polyamide is probably because water molecules surrounding –COOH or –SO3H groups prevent the firm fixing of polyamide macromolecules around SCQD or CCQD [50]. As a result, interfacial space would be produced and a looser polyamide structure can be obtained. Whereas, the introduction of NCQD endows the TFN-NCQD membranes with lower C/N ratio over the TFC membrane. This could be attributed to the amidation reaction between TMC and the BPEI on the surface of NCQD, which leads to a denser polyamide chemical structure [52]. In order to further verify the structure difference of these resultant TFN-CQD membranes, the MWCOs were further detected by neutral solute filtration using PEGs. The MWCO value is determined by the molecular weight of PEG molecules that are rejected by the membrane to 90% [22,23]. The retentions for the five different PEG fractions are presented in Fig. S4. From which, one can find that the MWCOs of the TFC, TFN-CCQD, TFN-NCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes are about 421, 494, 332 and 568 Da, respectively. It means that the selective layer becomes looser for TFN-SCQD and TFN-CCQD membranes and denser for TFN-NCQD membranes, which is consistent with the XPS results.

The surface morphologies and structures of the as-prepared membranes are observed by AFM and FESEM. Clearly, the surface of the PSf supporting membrane is relatively smooth (Fig. S5), while the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes exhibit a nodular structure packed by spherical globules ( Fig. 6), just like the typical structure of the polyamide layer obtained by interfacial polymerization using TMC and PIP [21,53]. AFM was also conducted to confirm the specific surface roughness of these membranes and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 7, Fig. S5 and Table 2. Clearly, the formation of polyamide layer on PSf supporting membranes sharply increases the roughness of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes. The TFC membrane has a R rms (root mean square roughness) of 37.9 nm and R a (average roughness) of 29.7 nm. After the incorporation of NCQD, the surface becomes slightly smoother with a R rms of 33.1 nm. Nevertheless, the introduction of SCQD or CCQD leads to a much rougher surface with a R rms of 40.0 nm for TFN-SCQD membrane and 50.8 nm for TFN-CCQD membrane, respectively. These interesting phenomena could be explained by the different diffusion rate and the initial position of PIP monomers [47,52]. For the fabrication of TFC membranes, PIP monomers with small molecular weight can easily permeate through the interface of aqueous and organic phase, resulting in a rough surface of polyamide layer. As for the TFN-NCQD membrane, PIP monomers face greater resistance to diffuse across the interface due to the electrostatic repulsion between cationic PIP and cationic groups –NH2 around NCQD. The steric hindrance of NCQD also further decreases the diffusion of PIP and retards the formation of polyamide layer [54]. As a result, a smoother surface is observed for TFN-NCQD membrane. Whereas, significantly different results are obtained for TFN-CCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes. This probably originates from the different chemical composition of different CQDs. To be specific, the presence of anionic groups on CCQD or SCQD enables the adsorption of a certain amount of cationic PIP monomers on their surfaces; This results in an increased PIP concentration around CQDs on the membrane surface, not just below their surface pores. In other words, the PIP molecules adsorbed on CQDs above the membrane surface are located in the deep of organic phase, which enlarges the reaction region of two immiscible phases between the aqueous phase and the organic phase and consequently leads to the formation of increased nodular morphology [32,55,56].

The water contact angle and surface charge of the prepared membranes are presented in Fig. 8. In comparison with the TFC membrane, the contact angle obviously decreased for all CQD-incorporated TFN membranes. The presence of numerous oxygen-containing groups or amino groups on CQDs is believed to endow the TFN membranes with excellent hydrophilicity, which can be clearly verified from the significantly higher contents of oxygen atom from the XPS results of these TFN-CQD membranes. Whereas, the hydrophilicity of TFN membranes incorporated with different CQDs doesn’t show any obvious difference [57]. In terms of surface charge properties, all the membranes are found to have a negative charge under about pH = 6, which is strongly linked to the carboxyl groups generated from hydrolysis of acyl chloride groups. More negative zeta potential values are observed for TFN-CCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes, which should be attributed to the anionic carboxyl groups or sulfonate groups on the surface of CCQD or SCQD. Especially, the TFN-SCQD membrane shows the most negatively charged surface due to the existence of multivalent anionic sulfonate groups. Accordingly, the introduction of NCQD makes the polyamide layer less negatively charged due to the presence of cationic BPEI on its surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeation and separation properties of membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9a shows the pure water flux at different pressures of the TFC and TFN-CQD membranes. It is found that the water fluxes increase linearly with the applied pressure for all membranes, indicating a stable NF system. The water flux of TFC membranes at the operation pressure of 0.6 MPa, based on the linear fit of the experimental data, is 18.0 L m−2 h−1. In comparison with the TFC membrane, all TFN-CQD membranes present a significantly improved water flux. The separation performance of these resultant TFN-CQD membranes also demonstrates comparable advantages over most of TFN membranes reported in the previous works (Table S2). This common increase for all the TFN-CQD membranes should be attributed to the common reasons. On one hand, the CQDs can be evenly dispersed in aqueous solution due to its nature of excellent water dispersibility. This is the crucial precondition of obtaining a high-performance TFN membrane. On the other hand, the CQDs can endow the polyamide layer with greatly improved hydrophilicity. According to the solution-diffusion theory, the increased hydrophilicity of membranes can greatly reduce the flow resistance and facilitate water solubilization and diffusion through polyamide layers [58]. Besides, some nanochannels between the CQD flakes as well as the unavoidably introduced voids between the interfaces of CQDs and polyamide matrix can also provide additional flow paths for water molecules to pass through the membranes [59,60].

It is noteworthy that the water fluxes of the TFN membranes incorporated with different CQDs also show obvious differences. At the operation pressure of 0.6 MPa, the SCQD endows the TFN-SCQD membrane with the highest water flux at 42.1 L m−2 h−1. The lowest water flux is obtained for NCQD, which leads to a water flux of 31.5 L m−2 h−1. For the TFN-CCQD membrane, it exhibits a water flux of 36.5 L m−2 h−1. This interesting observation makes us firmly believe that the functional groups of CQDs do play a significant role in elevating the permeability of TFN membranes [52]. To be specific, the chemical structure of polyamide is effected by the different functional groups on CQDs. In terms of the TFN-SCQD membrane, both SCQD and polyamide macromolecules could arrange in a regular formation and form a relatively loose structure due to the presence of –SO3H groups on SCQD, which is confirmed by XPS results discussed above [38,50]. Thus, the TFN membranes incorporated with SCQD would provide more transport paths for water molecules and consequently lead to a high water flux. Such explanations are also suited to the TFN-CCQD membrane. According to the XPS and MWCO results as discussed above, the crosslinking degree of polyamide is less affected by carboxyl groups in comparison with sulfonate acid groups. Therefore, the water flux of TFN-CCQD membrane is lower than that of TFN-SCQD membrane. On the contrary, the introduction of NCQD leads to a denser polyamide chemical structure due to the possible reactions between TMC and BPEI on the surface of NCQD. As a result, the TFN-NCQD membrane shows relatively lower water flux in comparison with TFN-SCQD or TFN-CCQD membranes.

Four types of salts (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, NaCl) are used to explore the NF separation performance of the prepared TFC and TFN membranes under an operation pressure of 0.6 MPa at pH = 6. As shown in Fig. 9b, all the membranes exhibit the highest rejection of ~ 94% to Na2SO4. All the membrane show the lowest rejection when NaCl is used as feed solution. The rejection behavior of all the membranes obeys the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl, which greatly corresponds to the negatively charged NF membranes [61,62]. The synergistic effect of size sieving and Donnan exclusion can be applied to explain these rejection results [63–65]. The residual carboxyl groups and other introduced negatively charged groups endow the surface of polyamide layer with negative charges. These negatively charged groups on the membrane surface could attract a high-valent cation and repulse a high-valent anion. Meanwhile, the hydrated radii of Mg2+, SO4 2-, Na+, and Cl- are 4.28, 3.79, 3.58 and 3.32 Å, respectively. Therefore, a high rejection to Na2SO4 and MgSO4 and a relatively low rejection to MgCl2 are achieved driven by the combination of Donnan exclusion and steric hindrance. The lowest NaCl retention for all membranes should be attributed to the assembled interfacial paths, which not only allow the permeation of water but also can let the penetration of small chloride ions.

Besides, the different rejection rates of the same salts can be observed for the TFC membrane and the TFN membranes incorporated with different CQDs. In terms of MgCl2 and NaCl, the TFN-CCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes show an obvious reduction in their retention compared to the TFC membranes, which is mainly due to the formation of a relatively looser and more negatively charged polyamide layer. Whereas, TFN-NCQD membranes diametrically show an increase in the rejection of MgCl2 or NaCl, which is originated from the decreased negative charge density and the improved crosslinking degree of the polyamide layer of the TFN-NCQD membranes [66]. When it comes to the rejection rates of Na2SO4 or MgSO4, all the membranes show similar values. This is mainly because that both Na2SO4 and MgSO4 contain SO4 2- which is multivalent anion and has the largest hydrated radius. As a result, the effect of electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance is strong enough to hold back of SO4 2-, thus resulting in high rejection rates of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 for all these different NF membranes.

Fouling is one of the unavoidable issues of NF membranes, which usually diminishes the membrane performance and ultimately shortens membrane lifetime [67,68]. To evaluate the antifouling performance of the as-prepared membranes, BSA is chosen as the model foulant and the cyclic filtration experiments are conducted. As shown in Fig. 10a, the permeation flux of all the tested membranes dramatically decreases when BSA is added to the feed solutions. While for the TFN-SCQD and TFN-CCQD membranes, the flux decline is much less pronounced in comparison with the TFC membrane. After a 20 h fouling test, the fluxes reach steady values. Subsequently, the fouled membranes are rinsed, while the permeation flux of all the membranes can’t recover their initial values due to the obstinate fouling [69].

To quantitatively assess the antifouling performance more specifically, the flux recovery ratio (FRR) and total fouling ratio (R t) are calculated and presented in Fig. 10b. The TFC membrane exhibits the lowest FRR of 57.9% whereas the R t is as high as 64.2%, indicating a severe BSA fouling. In comparison, all the TFN-CQD membranes show enhanced FRR and decreased R t. These results demonstrate that the incorporation of CQDs remarkably improves the antifouling properties of the membranes. This should be attributed to the increased surface hydrophilicity originated from the introduction of CQDs, which can reduce the adsorption of hydrophobic BSA on the membrane surface and also promote the desorption of BSA from the surface during the rinsing process [70]. Among these membranes, the TFN-CCQD and TFN-SCQD membranes show a better antifouling capacity. Especially, the TFN-SCQD membrane presents the best antifouling performance against BSA. Its FRR is as high as 87.6%, and the R t considerably decreases to 23.7%. Since the surface of TFN-SCQD membrane is more negatively charged due to the incorporation of additional negatively charged groups (-SO3 2-) on SCQD. As BSA is also negatively charged under pH = 7, the electrostatic repulsion between the surface of TFN-SCQD and BSA is much stronger, leading to the best antifouling performance [71]. Therefore, although the surface hydrophilicity of TFN-NCQD increases, its antifouling capacity is inconspicuously improved due to the less negatively charged surface.

In summary, three functionalized CQDs with carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups respectively, were synthesized and further incorporated into the polyamide layer of NF membranes via interfacial polymerization. The influences of the functional groups of CQDs on the membrane properties such as chemical structure, separation performance, antifouling capacity and so on, were systematically investigated. In comparison with a reference TFC membrane, the incorporation of CQDs resulted in a higher permeate flux along with well-maintained Na2SO4 rejection efficiency for all the TFN membranes. Besides, all these TFN-CQD membranes showed excellent antifouling capacity. Nonetheless, the different functional groups on CQDs also endowed the TFN with different properties. The TFN-SCQD membrane possessed the highest permeate flux of 42.1 L m−2 h−1 and the Na2SO4 rejection of 93.6% due to the formation of looser polyamide chemical structure and more negatively charged membrane surface, which also endowed the TFN-SCQD membrane with the best antifouling performance. The TFN-NCQD membrane exhibited a better retention of divalent cations than the other TFN membranes on the basis of better adhesion strength of amino groups of NCQD with the polyamide matrix and the less negatively charged membrane surface. Collectively, this study reveals that the properties, especially separation performance, of TFN membranes can be effectively modulated through tuning the functional groups of CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Ministry of Science & Technology of China (No. 2016YFA0203302).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2018.07.044.
